nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving forested streams and canals with mud or sand bottoms, soft to moderately hard water, and ample vegetation or submerged wood. Inhabits coastal-plain freshwater systems from southern Guinea through Sierra Leone to central Liberia; older literature also reports occurrences further east (Cameroon, Nile basin), but those records are now generally attributed to other Rubricatochromis or Hemichromis species and considered unconfirmed.

decorPreferences: sandy substrate (fish dig vigorously, especially when breeding)
flat rocks or slate slabs as spawning sites
clay flower pots and rockwork caves as retreats
rock stacks and driftwood to break lines of sight
robust epiphyte plants (Anubias, Java fern, Java moss) attached to hardscape rather than rooted plants

diet.notes: Generally accepts most quality prepared foods (cichlid pellets, sinking granules, flake) and benefits from regular supplementation with frozen or live foods such as bloodworm, brine shrimp, daphnia, mysis, and tubifex. Has been reported to develop more intense red coloration on a diet rich in carotenoid-containing live or frozen items. Vegetable matter (spirulina-enriched flakes, blanched spinach) is also taken and is commonly considered part of a balanced diet.

goodWith: robust open-water tetras such as Congo tetras
other similarly sized West African cichlids only in very large tanks with ample cover
outside of breeding, similarly sized robust mid-water fish
avoidWith: small or slow-moving tankmates (tetras, rasboras, livebearers, nano fish — likely to be killed during breeding)
long-finned fish such as angelfish or fancy guppies (fin nipping reported)
dwarf shrimp and other small invertebrates
other cichlids in tanks under ~250 L
any tankmates in the same aquarium once a pair is actively spawning, unless the tank is very large and well-aquascaped

imageCandidates.notes: Klaus Rudloff, 1600x1067. Striking full-body side view of a vibrant red-orange adult densely covered in iridescent blue-green spangles, with clear opercular and midbody spots and blue-spangled fins. Small corner attribution watermark; does not obscure the fish.
Photographer not listed on Commons, 1124x666. Sharp full lateral view of a colourful specimen showing the two diagnostic dark spots and blue spangling; whole animal in frame.
Zhyla, 670x608. Sharp head close-up showing the blue facial reticulation and opercular ocellus; tail cropped, kept as a diagnostic feature extra.

summary: The Jewel Cichlid (Rubricatochromis bimaculatus) is a brilliantly red-orange West African cichlid covered in iridescent blue spangles. Adaptable and easy to feed, it is nonetheless highly territorial — pairs escalate their aggression sharply when spawning and are widely considered dangerous to most tankmates during that period.
careNotes:
The Jewel Cichlid is a broadly adaptable West African cichlid from forested coastal streams of Guinea, Sierra Leone, and Liberia. In the aquarium it is generally considered intermediate in difficulty: water chemistry is forgiving (pH ~6.0–7.8, gH 4–18, 22–27 °C), but its temperament makes tank selection and stocking the real challenge.

Although often advertised at 30–40 gal in the trade, sources generally advise a tank of at least ~200 L (about 50–55 gal) with a 120 cm footprint for a single pair, with substantially more space if tankmates are intended. The species' adult length (commonly 10–13 cm, with reports up to 15 cm) and its all-tank territorial defense when breeding both push the recommendation upward. A long, low-profile tank is preferred over tall designs.

Decor should provide both line-of-sight breaks and clearly defined territories: a sandy substrate (fish dig actively when preparing to spawn), flat rocks or slates as potential spawning sites, clay flower pots as caves, and rock stacks or driftwood tangles. Rooted plants are typically uprooted; epiphytes attached to hardscape fare much better.

Outside of breeding, the species is moderately aggressive and territorial. Once a pair forms and spawning begins, aggression escalates sharply — small or slow tankmates are likely to be killed, and even larger robust fish can be harassed into hiding. Many keepers find a species-only setup with a single bonded pair to be the most practical arrangement. Long-finned tankmates may be fin-nipped.

Not to be confused with the related Lifalili Jewel (Rubricatochromis lifalili), which is distinguished by three black spots along the flank, or with the smaller Turkana / Dwarf Jewel Cichlid (R. exsul, recorded separately as fw-fish-480), which tends to be more docile and tops out around 10 cm. R. bimaculatus is the larger 'red jewel' that was long traded under the older name Hemichromis bimaculatus.

Taxonomic note: In 2022, a published revision split the old broad genus Hemichromis into two groups — Hemichromis (the five-spotted cichlids) and the newly named Rubricatochromis (the red jewel cichlids) — placing this species in the latter. The older name Hemichromis bimaculatus remains the name most aquarium sources still use.
breedingNotes:
Has been reported to breed readily once a pair forms — biparental open-substrate spawner. Aquarists commonly raise a group of 5–6 juveniles together and let pairs form naturally; trying to force-pair adults often produces severe aggression. Males tend to intensify in red colouration as breeding approaches, and the chosen spawning site (a cleaned flat rock, slate, or clay surface) is defended vigorously.

Females typically deposit several hundred adhesive eggs (reports range up to 500–600 per clutch). Eggs generally hatch in 2–4 days at 25–27 °C; wrigglers become free-swimming after a further 3–4 days. Both parents guard eggs and fry for about four weeks, attacking anything that approaches — in this period a small tankmate is very likely to be killed regardless of prior compatibility.

First foods for fry are freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii, microworms, and finely crushed flake. Parental care is intense and fry survival in a dedicated breeding tank is typically high. Outside dedicated breeding setups, removing eggs to a separate rearing tank is often necessary to protect other inhabitants from the parents.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hemichromis bimaculatus (Jewel Cichlid)
primary.notes: Aquarium Co-Op (designated slice primary) has no species page for Jewel Cichlid; swapped SeriouslyFish to primary per playbook fallback rule. SeriouslyFish provided water parameters (22–28 °C, pH 6.0–7.8, dH 4–18), adult size (15 cm), tank minimum (110 L / 120 × 30 × 30 cm), West African range (Guinea through Liberia), temperament (highly aggressive especially when spawning), breeding behaviour, and diet.

additional.name: FishBase — Rubricatochromis bimaculatus
additional.notes: Confirmed accepted name Rubricatochromis bimaculatus (Gill, 1862), family Cichlidae, order Cichliformes, West African distribution (Guinea, Sierra Leone, Liberia plus some unconfirmed records). Max size 13.6 cm SL. Water 21–23 °C, pH 6.5–7.5, dH 4–16. Confirmed reclassification from Hemichromis.

additional.name: Aquarium Source — Jewel Cichlid Care
additional.notes: Cross-check: ~15 cm captive size, 24–27 °C, pH 6.5–7.5, ~5–7 year lifespan, 40 gal (~150 L) minimum for a single fish. Emphasised that aggression escalates sharply during mating and that fin nipping of long-finned tankmates has been reported.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Jewel Cichlid
additional.notes: Cross-check: 10–13 cm, 22–28 °C, pH 6.0–7.8, dH 4–18, ~115 L minimum for a pair (200 L+ if community), up to ~500 eggs per spawn, both parents guard fry.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Rubricatochromis: a new genus for the red jewel cichlids
additional.notes: Taxonomic source. Documented the 2022 Lamboj & Koblmüller revision (Hydrobiologia) splitting Hemichromis sensu lato into Hemichromis (five-spotted jewels) and Rubricatochromis (red jewels), with R. bimaculatus as the type-like 'red jewel' species. Used to confirm alsoKnownAs and the careNotes taxonomic paragraph.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been reported to breed readily for a cichlid. It is a biparental open-substrate spawner that deposits eggs on flat rocks, slates, or other cleaned hard surfaces. The most commonly recommended approach is to raise a group of 5–6 juveniles together and allow a pair to form naturally. Aggression escalates dramatically once spawning starts — small tankmates in the same aquarium are likely to be killed.
